coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of pesticide based on split aptamer and magnetic separation
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ying
Zhu, Fuyan
Yin, Li
Qu, Guangxu
Ma, Dik-Lung
Leung, Chung-Hang
Lu, Lihua
coredata.dc:description: Pesticide residues have seriously threatened human health and affected the sustainable development of the environment. It is pivotal to establish an efficient detection platform for pesticide assays. A novel ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for sensitive determination of pesticide has been successfully established. Carbon dots (CDs) are used as the reference emitter, while the T-rich single-stranded DNA templated copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) provide the increasing signal. More precisely, CDs/magnetic beads (MBs) combined with split aptamer are used in this detection platform to present a label-free and enzyme-free isocarbophos (ICP) detection with high sensitivity and specificity. In the presence of ICP, a sandwich-typed complex is formed which attaches a T-rich DNA. After magnetic separation and addition of Cu2+/ascorbic acid (AA), the CuNPs are generated and the strong red luminescent appears. Without ICP, just blue emission from CDs exits. The developed ratiometric aptasensor offers an excellent ICP detection in the linear range of 3–200 nM with a detection limit of 0.81 nM. It provides new ways for the assay of pesticides in agricultural products or environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Split aptamer
CuNPs
Carbon dot
Ratiometric
Pesticide
Aptasensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of ICP using the ratiometric detection platform
Analysis of ICP in real samples
Detection principle of the ratiometric fluorescent ICP assay
Analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescent ICP detection
Selectivity, anti-interference and reproducibility of the ratiometric fluorescent ICP assay
Real sample detection ability of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent ICP aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The excitation and emission spectra of CDs, MB/CD and MB/CD@SiNP, (B) The XPS of (a) CDs, (b) MB/CD, and (c) MB/CD@SiNP, (C) The FT-IR spectra of (a) MBs, (b) CDs, (c) MB/CD and (d) MB/CD@SiNP. (D) Zeta potentials of MBs, CDs, MB/CD and MB/CD@SiNP, error bars represent the standard deviations (SDs) of the results from three independent experiments.

Feasibility of the ratiometric ICP detection platform. (A) The absorbance spectra of (a) DNA AP1 (b) MB/CD@SiNP (c) MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 (B) TEM image of the produced CuNPs in the presence of ICP. (C) The excitation and emission spectra of CuNPs formed in the detection system. (D) The fluorescent spectra of the precipitate systems were added Cu2+/AA, respectively, (a) MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 (b) MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 +AP2 (c) MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 +AP2 + ICP.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of the developed detection platform to various concentrations of ICP (0, 3, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 500, 1000, 2000 and 3000 nM) in buffered solution. (B) The liner relation between the concentrations (3, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150 and 200 nM) of ICP with the value of I 600/I 440, error bars represent the SDs of the results from three independent experiments, (C) The fluorescence changes corresponding to different concentrations (0, 10, 50, 100, 200 and 1000 nM) of ICP under the naked-eye observation (λEx=365 nm).

The selectivity (blue bar) and anti-interference (black bar) experiments of the ratiometric ICP detection platform. The concentrations of methidathion, imidacloprid, chlorpyrifos, phoxim, thiram, diethofencarb, atrazine, and metalaxyl are 1 μM and the concentration of ICP is 200 nM, error bars represent the SDs of the results from three independent experiments.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of the ratiometric detection platform to various concentrations of ICP (0, 3, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 500, 1000, 2000 and 3000 nM) in cabbage extract. (B) The liner relation between the concentrations (3, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150 and 200 nM) of ICP with the value of I 600/I 440, error bars represent the SDs of the results from three independent experiments.
Schematical prescription of the ratiometric fluorescent ICP assay’s principle.
Two strands of DNA sequences involved in this work.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a The bold and italic letters in AP1 and AP2 represent the aptamer sequence of isocarbophos.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of ICP spiked in apple or tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of pesticide based on split aptamer and magnetic separation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Macao, China, 999078

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pesticide residues have seriously threatened human health and affected the sustainable development of the environment. It is pivotal to establish an efficient detection platform for pesticide assays. A novel ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for sensitive determination of pesticide has been successfully established. Carbon dots (CDs) are used as the reference emitter, while the T-rich single-stranded DNA templated copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) provide the increasing signal. More precisely, CDs/magnetic beads (MBs) combined with split aptamer are used in this detection platform to present a label-free and enzyme-free isocarbophos (ICP) detection with high sensitivity and specificity. In the presence of ICP, a sandwich-typed complex is formed which attaches a T-rich DNA. After magnetic separation and addition of Cu2+/ascorbic acid (AA), the CuNPs are generated and the strong red luminescent appears. Without ICP, just blue emission from CDs exits. The developed ratiometric aptasensor offers an excellent ICP detection in the linear range of 3–200 nM with a detection limit of 0.81 nM. It provides new ways for the assay of pesticides in agricultural products or environmental samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor based on split aptamer and magnetic separation for highly sensitive detection of isocarbophos was successfully fabricated.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) have played a crucial role in agricultural industry. However, the abuse of OPPs has become widespread, which causes environmental pollutant, and ultimately enters human body to heavily destroy its nerve system [1,2]. In addition, a large amount of pesticides accumulated in human body can cause death [3,4]. Thus, exploiting highly efficient pesticides residue detection in complex solution has become a pivotal task to ensure human being’s safety and healthy life.

Apart from the traditionally used chromatography or enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA)-based OPPs detection methods [5–7], many kinds of novel biosensors have popped up and attracted great attention. To fabricate a typical biosensor, three key elements (recognition unit, signal transducer and signal output) are essential. Aptamer is a specially synthesized DNA/RNA oligonucleotides and its sequence is determined by exponential enrichment techniques [8,9]. It is one of the recognition elements of biosensors having the advantages of high specificity, strong affinity, high stability and easy synthesis. These properties make it the most potential biosensor composite for multi-pesticides detection [10–12]. Considering the high specificity and sensitivity of the sandwich-typed biosensor based on the dual recognition principle, Stojanovic et al. [13] develop the concept of split aptamer. Split aptamer can be obtained by cutting the original aptamer into two parts without changing their recognition ability. In the recognition process, two parts of aptamer with the target can then be formed into a sandwich-typed aptamer/target/aptamer complex through self-assembly [14]. When the small target has a long chain oligonucleotide aptamer, the split aptamer technique’s advantage is reflected more obviously since split aptamer not only overcomes the existing steric hindrance of the original long chain aptamer, but also makes the aptamer synthesis easier. The second element is the signal transducer. In a typical aptamer-based biosensor, the signal transducer is usually acted by the configuration changes of aptamer DNA which is aroused by the recognition of target/aptamer.

The third element of a biosensor is the signal output. Fluorescence-based biosensors stand out among various signal outputs due to their simple operation, high stability and sensitivity aptamer [15,16]. The copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) synthesized from DNA templates have demonstrated their advantages of facile preparation, large Stokes shift, strong emission and low cost [17–20]. It is reported that single-stranded poly T DNA can facilitate the formation of CuNPs in the presence of Cu2+/ascorbic acid (AA) through acting as the NPs’ template. More importantly, CuNPs possess the unique merit of low background, even zero-background when the detection platforms are intelligently designed. In this case, magnetic separation is used to assist the realization of the zero-background of CuNPs. Once the DNA template is removed from the detection system through magnetic separation, then there will be no CuNP generated because of lacking template, so zero-background is obtained [21]. Fabricating a zero-background biosensor is essential in fluorescence-based detection since it can significantly increase the biosensor’s sensitivity. Fe3O4 magnetic beads (MBs) are the most commonly used and easy to get magnetic material, so they have been widely used in magnetic separations [22].

Moreover, the mono signal change in fluorescence detection is easy to be interfered by the external conditions. The ratiometric detection can overcome this shortcoming, and it can self-correct the detection inaccuracy using two different fluorescent signals [23–26]. Carbon dots (CDs) are highly emitted nanoparticles having the advantages of adjusted emission intensity and emission wavelength, high stability and high quantum yield [27]. Especially, their emission peaks are usually high and narrow which is a unique property. CDs have become a promising candidate to act as reference signal since the narrow peak will not disturb the other signal emission peaks in a multi-signal fluorescent biosensor [28–30].

Enlightened by these novel concepts, this study aims at developing a novel ratiometric fluorescent pesticide aptasensor. The CDs provide reference signal and the T-rich DNA templated CuNPs offer increased signal. The magnetic separation and split aptamer technique are used to manufacture a low background and switch-on ratiometric detection platform. Isocarbophos (ICP) is one of the widely employed OPPs. It has a strong insecticidal ability on mites in lepidoptera, arachnida and homoptera. Therefore, it is used as example to exhibit the exquisitely designed ratiometric biosensor.

ICP (98.6%, purity) was bought from TCI (Shanghai, China). Other pesticides, including chlorpyrifos, thiram, methidathion, imidacloprid, phoxim, diethofencarb, metalaxyl, and atrazine were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). AA, 3-(N-morpholino)-propane sulfonic acid (MOPS), FeCl3, citric acid, ethylenediamine, and CuSO4 were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The two strands of oligonucleotide involved in this work are listed in Table 1, which were synthesized by Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). They were dissolved in Milli-Q water to get the stock solution with the concentration of 100 μM.

The preparations of Fe3O4 MBs and CDs were presented in Electronical Supplementary Material (ESM). MB/CD@SiNP was prepared according to a previous method [29]. 600 μL of CDs solution and 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 2.0 mg)/N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 2.0 mg) were added in 8 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 30 min in the dark. NH2-modified Fe3O4 MBs (10 mg) was then added into this mixture and continuously stirred for 12 h at room temperature. The covalently linked complex MB/CD was collected through centrifugation, and the obtained precipitate was washed with ultrapure water, which was redispersed in ultrapure water (10 mg·mL–1) for further use. Finally, MB/CD@SiNP was fabricated through doping MB/CD into silicane nanoparticles (SiNPs), and it was further NH2-modified by APTES. Specifically, MB/CD (500 μL) aqueous solution was added into the mixture of anhydrous ethanol (4 mL), ultrapure water (200 μL), APTES (100 μL) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 100 μL). Then, ammonia water (400 μL) was slowly dropped into the reactant and continuously oscillated overnight at room temperature. Afterwards, the NH2-modified MB/CD@SiNP was separated through centrifugation and alternatively washed with ultrapure water\anhydrous ethanol three times. The NPs were frozen-dried in vacuum and dispersed in super pure water (1.0 mg·mL–1) for next use.

The modification of the one-part ICP aptamer AP1 onto the surface of MB/CD@SiNP was performed according to the previous report [31]. COOH-modified AP1 (25 μL, 100 μM) was dispersed in 75 μL phosphate buffered sodium (PBS) buffer (0.1 M Na2HPO4, 0.1 M NaH2PO4, 50 mM NaCl, pH = 7.4), then EDC (10 mg) and NHS (5 mg) were added in the AP1-contained solution and kept at 37 ◦C for 2 h to activate the –COOH group. Afterwards, 200 μL of NH2-modified MB/CD@SiNP (1.0 mg·mL–1) was dropped into the above mixture and incubated at 37 ◦C for 6 h. The reactant was centrifugally separated (4000 r·min–1, 3 min) and washed with water several times to get rid of the possible impurities. The pure MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 probe was gained and prepared into an aqueous solution (2.0 mg·mL–1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ICP using the ratiometric detection platform
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solution of ICP (1 mM) was prepared through ultrasonically dispersing 2.89 g ICP into 10 mL methanol, and the obtained solution was stored in –20 ℃ for further use. Firstly, AP2 (10 μM) was annealed through heating to 95 °C, kept at 95 °C for 5 min and then cooled to room temperature in an hour. Then, 10 μL of MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 (2.0 mg·mL–1), 15 μL of AP2 and different concentrations of ICP were incubated in the certain volume of binding buffer (50 mM NaCl, 10 mM KCl, 10 mM MgCl2, and 50 mM Tris, pH = 8.0) for 0.5 h at 25 °C to facilitate the combination between ICP with AP2 and MB/CD@SiNP-AP1[32]. After magnetic separation, the supernatant was removed, and the precipitate was dispersed in 10 μL aqueous solution. Then, Cu2+ (4 μL, 5 mM) and AA (6 μL, 50 mM) were added in the above solution to produce CuNPs. At the same time, MOPS buffer (10 mM MOPS, 150 mM NaCl, pH = 7.6) was also added into the mixture to result in a final volume of 100 μL. After incubating at 25 °C for 5 min in the dark, the fluorescent spectra of these prepared samples were tested through scanning the fluorescence wavelength range from 360 to 640 nm with an excitation wavelength λex = 340 nm. The intensity ratios between λem = 440 nm with 600 nm were calculated to evaluate the signal change. The control experiments were carried out in the corresponding conditions just without the addition of ICP. All the fluorescent tests were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ICP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the platform was investigated using fresh cabbage, apple and tap water as real samples. The cabbage and apple were purchased from a local market (Qingdao, China). 5 g chopped cabbage or apple was first mixed with 25 mL of methanol, and then was stirred quickly in a beaker for 6 h. The processed samples were centrifuged (12,000 rpm, 15 min). The obtained supernatant was filtered by 0.22 µm membrane and for subsequent ICP detection. For each sample, the detection procedures were similar to those of the ICP detections performed in buffered solution.

The synthesis procedure of MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 was presented in Scheme S1. The separately prepared Fe3O4 MBs and CDs were covalently combined into MB/CD through amide bond. Then, MB/CD was wrapped up by silicane and subsequently modified with –NH2 to facilitate grafting one of the aptamer DNA AP1. Firstly, the photophysical properties of AP1-MB/CD@SiNP were investigated. Fig. 1 A a and b show that the synthesized CDs have the maximum excitation and emission at 340 nm and 440 nm, respectively. Once CDs are connected to the surface of Fe3O4 MBs, the excitation and emission peaks of CDs have no obvious change, while the fluorescence intensity of the CDs is partially quenched by MBs (Fig. 1 A c and d). After doping of MB/CD into silicane NPs, the fluorescence intensity has no obvious change (Fig. 1 A e and f). It is observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) that the prepared CDs are highly distributed spherical NPs with a narrow size distribution, indicating a better purity of the obtained CDs (Fig. S1A) [29]. When combining CDs with MBs, the obtained MB/CD appears as spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 300 nm (Fig. S1B). After MB/CD is encapsulated into silicane shell, the prepared MB/CD@SiNP particles still have the size distribution in the nanoscale level with an increased average diameter of 400 nm (Fig. S1C), showing the successful encapsulation of MB/CD [33,34]. The successful preparation of MB/CD@SiNPs-AP1 was proved by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), FT-IR spectra and Zeta potential. It can be seen from Fig. 1B a that there are three peaks at 284.5 eV, 401.0 eV and 532.0 eV, respectively [35], which are attributed to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s in CDs. In Fig. 1B b, an additional peak at 711.9 eV appears, which belongs to Fe 2p in Fe3O4 MBs, confirming the successful combination of MB with CD. When MB/CD@SiNP was fabricated through doping MB/CD into SiNPs, a new peak at 103.0 eV appears, which is attributed to Si 2p (Fig. 1B c), indicating that MB/CD@SiNP is successfully prepared. The high resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s and Fe 2p for MB/CD@SiNPs were presented in Fig. S2 A−C. The C 1 s peaks at 284.8, 286.3, 287.8 and 289.1 eV can be assigned to the chemical bonds of C–C, C–N, CO and O−CO, respectively (Fig. S2 A). The O 1 s spectrum contains peaks at 533.8 eV and 532.4 eV in Fig. S2 B, indicating that oxygen mostly exists in the states of CO and C–O, respectively [36]. Fig. S2 C shows that the high resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p are divided into three peaks at 710.0 eV, 719.0 eV and 725.0 eV, respectively. The peak at 719.0 eV is due to Fe3+ and the binding energies at 710.0 eV and 725.0 eV correspond to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, which are the typical characteristic peaks of Fe3O4 structure containing FeO and Fe2O3 [35]. Fig. 1 C presents the FT-IR spectra of MBs, CDs, MB/CD and MB/CD@SiNP. As shown in Fig. 1 C a, the peak at 561 cm−1 is due to the vibrations of Fe–O, and the bands at 3372 cm–1 and 1615 cm–1 are attributed to the N–H stretching vibration and bending vibration, respectively, verifying –NH2 groups are modified in the surface of Fe3O4 MBs [35]. In curve b, the peaks at 2943 cm–1 and 883 cm–1 are related to the stretching vibration and the bending vibrations of C–H in CDs, respectively. The absorption band around 3390 cm–1 is accounted for the stretching vibrations of O–H, and the peak at 1562 cm–1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO, showing the existence of –COOH in CDs [37]. In curve c, the peak at 1655 cm–1 is the characteristic peak of –CO–NH– vibration, indicating that CD is covalently attached on the surface of Fe3O4 MBs through the formation of amide bond between amino group and carboxyl group [38]. In curve d, the peak at 1093 cm–1 is attributable to the intense vibration of Si–O, showing the existence of SiNPs [39]. The peaks at 3372 cm–1 and 1617 cm–1 belong to the stretching and bending vibration of –NH since MB/CD@SiNP is also modified by amino groups. The Zeta potentials of Fe3O4 MBs, CDs, MB/CD and MB/CD@SiNPs under neutral conditions are tested as 16.2 mV, −9.6 mV, −0.9 mV and 23.1 mV, respectively (Fig. 1D). The surface of Fe3O4 MBs are covalently grafted by –NH2 groups, leading to a positive Zeta potential value. The CDs carry –COOH groups, and hence present a negative potential. Once CDs are connected to the surface of Fe3O4, the Zeta potential of MB/CD changes to −0.9 mV, indicating that the CDs have been attached onto Fe3O4. The Zeta potential of MB/CD@SiNP increases to 23.1 mV because the surface of MB/CD@SiNP is also covalently grafted by –NH2 groups. Additionally, the UV–visible absorption spectra of the prepared NPs were investigated. In Fig. S3, the CDs have UV absorption at 340 nm, while Fe3O4 MBs have a broad absorption band from 300 nm to 600 nm [40]. After grafting the CDs onto the surface of Fe3O4, MB/CD possesses the absorption band from 300 nm to 600 nm which contains the clear absorption peak of CDs at 340 nm, indicating that CDs has been attached onto Fe3O4 MB. After MB/CD was doped into SiNPs, the absorbance of MB/CD@SiNP shows a moderately week band from 300 to 600 nm which is caused by the relatively large diameter of SiNPs and the lower concentrations of MB/CD. The magnetism of Fe3O4 MB and MB/CD@SiNP have been exhibited in Fig. S4. It was found that both Fe3O4 MB and MB/CD@SiNP can easily disperse in aqueous solution and subsequently be separated completely from the solution by a magnet. All these results verify the successful fabrication of MB/CD@SiNP. The stabilities of CDs, MB/CD, and MB/CD@SiNP were studied through monitoring their fluorescence changes in buffered solution. Fig. S5 shows that these three nanomaterials emit stably in 20 days, indicating a high stability of these nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection principle of the ratiometric fluorescent ICP assay
ce:section.ce:para: The 54-base ICP aptamer was rationally split into two shorter strands (Fig. S6) [32,41]. One part of aptamer has 24 bases, named as AP1, which is attached onto the surface of MB/CD@SiNP. The other part of aptamer with 30 bases is contained by AP2 at its 3′-end. Meanwhile, 30 T bases DNA sequence is attached on the 5′-end of AP2. The successive T bases act as the signal probe which can generate CuNPs in the presence of Cu2+/AA. The detection principle was schematically demonstrated in Scheme 1. The initially blue-emitted MB/CD@SiNPs-AP1 was used as an internal reference for ratiometric fluorescent detection. In the presence of ICP, the AP1 attached on MB/CD@SiNP and free AP2 can undergo conformational change and transform into the sandwich-typed complex of MB/CD@SiNPs-AP1/ICP/AP2 via self-assembly. After magnetic separation, MB/CD@SiNPs-AP1/ICP/AP2 remains in the precipitate system. The subsequent addition of Cu2+/AA solution leads to the generation of strong emitted (λem = 600 nm) CuNPs, which is ascribed to the T-rich DNA contained by AP2, while the emission at λem = 440 nm keeps stable. Hence, a ratio metric ICP detection platform is fabricated. Without ICP, the sandwich complex MB/CD@SiNPs-AP1/ICP/AP2 cannot be formed. The T-rich sequence is removed by the after magnetic separation. Thus, no CuNPs are generated, leading to the non-existence of red fluorescence in the system.

Firstly, the successful preparation of MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 is vital. Therefore, the grafting of ssDNA AP1 onto the surface of MB/CD@SiNPs was proved by UV–visible absorption spectrum and Zeta potential. DNA AP1 has a UV absorption peak at 260 nm ( Fig. 2A a). Initially, MB/CD@SiNP only has weak absorption peak at 340 nm (Fig. 2A b). After grafting AP1 onto the surface of MB/CD@SiNP, MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 possesses the additional absorption peak of DNA at 260 nm (Fig. 2A c), indicating that AP1 has been attached onto MB/CD@SiNP. The Zeta potentials of MB/CD@SiNP and MB/CD @SiNPs-AP1 under neutral condition are tested as 19.1 mV and −8.9 mV, respectively (Fig. S7). The surface of MB/CD@SiNP is covalently grafted with –NH2 groups using APTES, leading to a positive potential. After being modified by AP1, MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 has a significant lower potential than that of NH2-modified MB/CD@SiNP since the attached DNA groups are negatively charged due to their phosphate groups. The formation of CuNPs is also important in this detection. In the presence of ICP, the T-rich DNA containing AP2 and AP1 is formed into the sandwich-typed complex AP1/ICP/AP2. Therefore, the formation of CuNPs in the detection condition is investigated. Using AP1/ICP/AP2 as the template, after 5 min incubating with AA/Cu2+, TEM was used to observe the resulting CuNPs (Fig. 2B). The produced NPs are very tiny with an average diameter of 2.0 nm, having a spherical shape and without obvious aggregation. Moreover, the generated CuNPs possess the maximal excitation and emission at 340 nm (Fig. 2C a) and 600 nm (Fig. 2C b), respectively, which is consistent with the reported DNA-templated CuNPs [42]. Finally, the luminescent responses toward several different detection conditions were tested to evaluate the feasibility of this detection platform. When the system just contains MB/CD@SiNP-AP1, after magnetic separation and the addition of Cu2+/AA, only a blue-emitted fluorescence was observed in the precipitate system which comes from CDs contained by MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 (Fig. 2D a). When the system contains MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 and AP2, after magnetic separation and the addition of Cu2+/AA, just blue fluorescence is still found (Fig. 2D b). On the contrary, once ICP is added to the system containing MB/CD@SiNP-AP1 and AP2, after magnetic separation and the addition of Cu2+/AA, the precipitate system shows an additional fluorescence peak at λem = 600 nm (Fig. 2D c). This is because MB/CD@SiNPs-AP1, ICP and AP2 can form into the complex MB/CD@SiNPs-AP1/ICP/AP2 through the affinity of aptamer towards target. After magnetic separation, T-rich sequences are remained in the precipitate system. So the inputting of Cu2+/AA can cause the formation of the poly(T) templated CuNPs, which results in strong emission. Additionally, the dissociation constant (K d) of ICP binding to AP1/AP2 is tested as 0.81 through microscale thermophoresis (MST) testing (Fig. S8), which is close to the K d of ICP with its integrity aptamer [43], verifying that the cutting does not obviously affect the interaction of ICP with its aptamer.

The ratiometric strategy can minimize the external influences such as probe concentration, pH, temperature and ionic strength. Hence, in this detection, only the concentrations of AP2, Cu2+, AA and the generation time of CuNPs, have been optimized. The specifical optimization steps are listed in ESM. Firstly, the concentration of AP2 is investigated since AP2 acts as the detection signal. Fig. S9A exhibits that the fluorescence intensity ratio of I 600/I 440 gets its plateau at 1.5 μM of AP2, which shows that the maximum CuNPs are produced in this condition. Secondly, the concentrations of Cu2+ and AA are optimized, respectively. Fig. S9B shows that the intensity ratio of I 600/I 440 reaches the maximum at 200 μM of Cu2+. As exhibited by Fig. S9C, once the concentration of AA increases from 1 mM to 5 mM, the value of I 600/I 440 reaches the maximum at 3 mM of AA. Lastly, the incubation time of CuNPs is optimized, as shown in Fig. S9D, and 5 min is the best time to generate CuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescent ICP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent spectra of the presented detection platform towards different concentrations of ICP were investigated. As the ICP concentrations climb from 0 to 3000 nM, the fluorescent intensity of CDs at λEm= 440 nm keeps stable, while the intensities of CuNPs at λEm= 600 nm gradually increase ( Fig. 3 A). The fluorescence intensity ratio I 600/I 440 reaches the vertex at the concentration of 3000 nM. Fig. 3B presents the linear relationship (y = 0.0197x + 0.5103, R2 = 0.995) between the values of I 600/I 440 with the ICP concentrations in the range of 3–200 nM. According to the linear relationship, the limit of detection (LOD) for ICP is estimated as 0.81 nM (3σ/slope) and the calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) value of I 600/I 440 for ten blank samples is 0.0053 (Table S2). To compare this study with the reported ICP detection methods, Table S1 presents the recently reported ICP assays with different strategies. It can be found that this ratiometric detection possesses a relatively lower LOD than most of the other fluorescent ICP detection ascribed to its self-correction property[32, 35, 44–47], while the proposed aptasensor is not comparable to the highly sensitive electrochemiluminescence [41] or chemiluminescence technique [48]. The detection linear range containing three orders of magnitude are comparable to the most other detection strategies. Taken together, the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor has a satisfactory detection performance, which is due to its ratiometric property and low background. Additionally, the detection systems with different concentrations of ICP can experience the emission color changes of blue-purple-red through the naked-eye observation (Fig. 3 C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, anti-interference and reproducibility of the ratiometric fluorescent ICP assay
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity and anti-interference of the proposed ratiometric detection for ICP assay is investigated by monitoring the value change of I 600/I 440 towards other commonly used OPPs, including methidathion (METH), imidacloprid (IMI), chlorpyrifos (CHL), phoxim (PHO), thiram (THI), diethofencarb (DIE), atrazine (ATR), and metalaxyl (META). Fig. 4 demonstrates that only ICP can increase the value of I 600/I 440. Neither the blue nor the red emission change can be found in the addition of other OPPs. Moreover, the interference experiments have been performed through mixing ICP with other OPPs. In the presence of other OPPs/ICP, no obvious change of I 600/I 440 value can be observed, indicating that there is no interference from other OPPs in ICP detection. The repeatability experiment was carried out through investigating 10 samples having the same concentration of ICP. As shown in Fig. S10, the RSD of the detection was calculated as 1.4, indicating a higher detection reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample detection ability of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent ICP aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practical application of the proposed ICP detection platform, it was applied to assay ICP in fresh cabbage, apple and tap water samples. The ICP detection was first performed in cabbage extract. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescent intensity at λEm = 600 nm gradually increases as the concentrations of ICP grow. In Fig. 5B, the changing of I 600/I 440 towards different ICP concentrations is presented, which shows the linear relation y = 0.0100x + 0.4432 (R2 = 0.997) in the concentration range of 3–200 nM. The obtained relation in cabbage extract is similar to that in the buffered solution, indicating potential application feasibility. Furthermore, the practicability of the detection platform was presented through evaluating the recovery rate of apple and tap water samples. As shown in Table 2, when 10 nM, 50 nM and 100 nM of ICP were sequentially titrated into the apple and tap water samples, respectively, the recovery ranges of these two real samples were obtained as 96.4–102.4% and 96.0–102.7%, respectively. The RSDs are exhibited as 1.5–4.7, showing that this ratiometric detection platform has a satisfactory accuracy.

In summary, a novel ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor has been developed to sensitively detect ICP, using CD as the reference signal and CuNPs as the increasing signal. The main property of this ratiometric detection platform resides in the applications of split aptamer and magnetic separation, which not only endows the detection with a switch-on signal and a low background, but also provides an enzyme-free and label-free ICP detection platform. Meanwhile, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor is relatively simple since it is just made up of CD dopped MBs, two strands of split aptamer DNA and Cu2+/AA, showing the merits of facile operation and cost-effective property. However, the developed method has a relatively narrow linear range and short signal amplification. These shortcomings could be further improved in future work by choosing a suitable reference signal or indicator signal to optimize the NPs’ composition, and employing oligonucleotides, enzymes or nanomaterials-based methods to design amplification strategies.

Ying Wang: Methodology, Validation, Software, Investigation, Writing − original draft, Data analysis and discussion. Fuyan Zhu: Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Software, Data curation. Li Yin: Validation, Data analysis and discussion. Guangxu Qu: Data analysis and discussion, Validation. Dik-Lung Ma: Data curation, Supervision. Chung-Hang Leung: Writing − review & editing, Project administration. Lihua Lu: Methodology, Resources, Writing − review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 21705089, 22077109), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (no. ZR2017MB064), Science and Technology Program of Qingdao (no. 18–6-1–83-nsh), and the Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (no. J17KA109).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132045.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ying Wang is currently a student for her Master degree under the supervision of Prof. Lihua Lu at Qingdao Agricultural University. Her research topic focuses on fluorescent biosensors for pesticide detection.
Fuyan Zhu is currently an undergraduate under the supervision of Prof. Lihua Lu at Qingdao Agricultural University. His research topic focuses on the development of luminescent biosensors.
Li Yin is currently a technician at the College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences in Qingdao Agricultural University. She is now acting as a research assistant in Prof Lu’s group.
Guangxu Qu is currently an undergraduate under the supervision of Prof. Lihua Lu at Qingdao Agricultural University. His research topic focuses on the development of luminescent biosensors.

Dik-Lung Ma completed his PhD in 2004 at the University of Hong Kong under the supervision of Prof. C.-M. Che. He spent the years 2005–2009 at the University of Hong Kong, the Hong Kong Polytechnic University, and the Scripps Research Institute with Prof. C.-M. Che, Prof. K-Y. Wong, and Prof. R. Abagyan. He is currently an Associate Professor at Hong Kong Baptist University. His research interests mainly focus on luminescent sensing of biomolecules and metal ions, computer-aided drug discovery, and inorganic medicine.

Chung-Hang Leung completed his PhD in 2002 at City University of Hong Kong. After completing a five year post-doctoral fellowship at the Department of Pharmacology, Yale University, he was appointed as a Research Assistant Professor at the University of Hong Kong and then at the Hong Kong Baptist University. He is currently appointed as a Professor at University of Macau. His primary research interests are in structure-based drug discovery and in the development of oligonucleotide-based drug screening methods.

Lihua Lu completed her PhD at Hong Kong Baptist University under the supervision of Dr. D.L. Ma. She is currently appointed as a Professor at the College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences in Qingdao Agricultural University. Her research interests include the development of luminescent probes and construction label-free detection platforms for various biomolecules or toxic small molecules.
